past_medical_history: No prior cardiac history or diagnoses
No prior syncope evaluations — all episodes were attributed to dehydration or viral illness
family_history: Father died suddenly at age 42 in the Philippines — cause of death unknown, occurred during sleep; no autopsy was performed
Older brother (age 41) had one episode of syncope during a febrile illness 3 years ago — never evaluated; lives in Manila
Mother alive at age 64, healthy, no cardiac history
No known family history of epilepsy, long QT syndrome, or cardiomyopathy

social_history.occupation: Registered nurse in the emergency department, works 12-hour shifts
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and 6-year-old daughter in a suburban home
social_history.exercise: Active lifestyle — plays basketball recreationally once per week, occasional jogging

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent with normal rate and prosody, no dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, serial 7s performed without error. Registration 3/3, recall 3/3 at 5 minutes. No confusion or disorientation. Patient is cooperative and provides coherent detailed history.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive 3mm to 2mm. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus, no diplopia. CN V: Sensation intact in all divisions bilaterally, masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no facial droop. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally. Weber midline, Rinne air > bone bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline, gag reflex intact bilaterally. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue protrudes midline, no atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout all extremities. No rigidity, spasticity, or fasciculations. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups tested (deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger extensors, grip, interossei, hip flexors, knee extensors, knee flexors, ankle dorsiflexors, ankle plantarflexors) bilaterally. No pronator drift. No tremor.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception intact throughout all four extremities. No sensory level. No dermatomal deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal stride length, base, and arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No ataxia or unsteadiness.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or lateral tongue bite marks. No evidence of urinary or fecal incontinence. Small abrasion on right forehead from fall during the syncopal episode. No bruising or signs of prolonged convulsive activity. No post-ictal features — patient was fully alert and oriented within 30-45 seconds of regaining consciousness.

patient.chief_complaint: Passed out at work during a fever
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Jerome Bautista is a 38-year-old right-handed Filipino-American man who works as an ER nurse, presenting after being found unresponsive in the break room at work. He was on shift and had been feeling unwell with influenza-like symptoms since the previous evening — subjective fevers, myalgias, headache, and sore throat. He checked his temperature at home that morning (38.8°C) but came to work as the department was short-staffed. Approximately 3 hours into his shift, coworkers found him slumped in a chair in the break room, unresponsive. They initiated BLS protocol and moved him to the floor. A coworker who was monitoring him reports that he was pulseless for approximately 5-8 seconds before regaining a pulse spontaneously. He was unconscious for approximately 30 seconds total and then woke up, appearing confused for only a few seconds before becoming fully alert and asking 'What happened?' No tonic-clonic activity was witnessed, though one coworker thought she saw brief 'twitching' of his arms lasting a few seconds. There was no tongue biting, no urinary incontinence, and no post-ictal confusion beyond the initial few seconds. On detailed questioning, Mr. Bautista reports 3 prior syncopal episodes over the past 5 years, all occurring during febrile illnesses: (1) Age 33, during a severe urinary tract infection with high fever — found on the bathroom floor by his wife, who estimated he was unconscious for less than a minute. (2) Age 35, during community-acquired pneumonia — witnessed collapse during ambulation on the ward (he was an inpatient), spontaneous recovery, attributed to orthostatic hypotension. (3) Age 37, during COVID-19 infection — his wife found him unconscious in bed and describes a 'terrifying' episode where she could not feel his pulse for what she estimates was 10-15 seconds before he gasped and regained consciousness. He was taken to the ER and had a normal ECG and normal troponin; the event was attributed to vasovagal syncope. Between febrile episodes, he has been entirely asymptomatic with no syncope, palpitations, chest pain, or exercise intolerance. He has no history of seizures. He has no cardiac history and takes no medications. He is particularly anxious because his father died suddenly at age 42 in the Philippines — the family was told it was a 'heart attack in his sleep,' but no autopsy was performed and no prior cardiac evaluation had been done. His older brother in Manila experienced a similar syncopal episode during a fever 3 years ago but was never evaluated.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive to eye opening and closure
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-amplitude beta activity, symmetric, no focal slowing
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness captured with vertex waves. No sleep spindles or K-complexes obtained in this study.

artifacts.description: Low-amplitude intermittent muscle artifact from jaw clenching, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric buildup of diffuse theta slowing as expected. No epileptiform discharges or focal features provoked. Normal recovery.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Symmetric photic driving response at 14-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform discharges, focal slowing, or electrographic seizures captured during this 40-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute study. Events were not captured during this recording. Sensitivity of a single routine EEG for detecting interictal epileptiform discharges is approximately 50%.

additional_observations: Normal brain parenchyma without atrophy, edema, or signal abnormality
Normal ventricular system and sulcal pattern for age
No abnormal enhancement after gadolinium administration
Normal MRA of the intracranial circulation
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast. No structural abnormality identified to explain the patient's syncopal episodes. Normal intracranial MRA. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Normal sinus rhythm at 88 bpm
Normal P-wave morphology and axis
Normal PR interval (168 ms)
Normal QRS duration (92 ms)
Normal QTc interval (408 ms)

Subtle ST changes in V1-V2: slight ST elevation with a saddle-back morphology (type 3 or inconclusive Brugada-like pattern) — these changes are equivocal and could represent a normal variant, early repolarization, or an incomplete Brugada pattern
No Brugada type 1 coved pattern
No epsilon waves
No pre-excitation (no delta waves)
Normal R-wave progression across precordial leads
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 88 bpm. Normal intervals throughout. In isolation, this ECG would be read as normal or nonspecific.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 11.4 x10^9/L (H), CRP 32 mg/L (H), Influenza A rapid antigen Positive qualitative (H).

findings: Normal sinus rhythm at 92 bpm
TYPE 1 BRUGADA PATTERN now present: coved-type ST-segment elevation ≥2 mm (2.5 mm measured) in leads V1 and V2, with a downsloping ST segment merging into inverted T waves
This is the pathognomonic Brugada type 1 (coved) ECG pattern
The pattern was NOT present on the initial ECG obtained at temperature 38.6°C; it has now appeared at peak fever of 39.4°C
Normal QTc interval (408 ms) — Long QT syndrome excluded
No epsilon waves (argues against ARVC)
No pre-excitation
No other conduction abnormalities
output.interpretation: The Type 1 pattern that appears spontaneously (even if fever-provoked) carries the same prognostic significance as a baseline Type 1 pattern.

output.rhythm: Normal sinus rhythm at baseline, progressing to Brugada pattern during infusion

intervals.PR: 178 ms (baseline 168 ms — mildly prolonged during ajmaline)
intervals.QRS: 108 ms (baseline 92 ms — widened during ajmaline due to sodium channel blockade)

findings: Baseline ECG (afebrile, temperature 36.8°C): normal sinus rhythm, subtle saddle-back ST changes in V1-V2 (unchanged from initial presentation), non-diagnostic
Ajmaline infusion (1 mg/kg IV over 5 minutes): at minute 3, ST segment in V1 begins to elevate with coved morphology
At minute 5: definitive Type 1 Brugada pattern in V1, V2, and now V3 — coved ST elevation ≥2 mm (3.0 mm in V1, 2.5 mm in V2, 2.0 mm in V3) with negative T waves
QRS widened to 108 ms (25% increase from baseline), consistent with sodium channel blockade
PR interval prolonged to 178 ms
No ventricular arrhythmia provoked during the infusion
ECG changes resolved 25 minutes after completion of ajmaline infusion
Test interpreted as POSITIVE — diagnostic of Brugada syndrome
output.interpretation: Ajmaline provocation test is POSITIVE. After afebrile resolution, the resting ECG returned to a non-diagnostic pattern. This is concordant with the fever-provoked Type 1 pattern observed during the index admission.

signal_characteristics.cine: Normal biventricular size, wall thickness, and systolic function. No regional wall motion abnormalities. Normal valvular function.
signal_characteristics.T1_mapping: Normal native T1 values throughout the myocardium
signal_characteristics.T2_mapping: Normal T2 values, no myocardial edema
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: No late gadolinium enhancement identified — no myocardial fibrosis, scarring, or infiltrative disease

additional_observations: No evidence of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, dilated cardiomyopathy, or myocarditis
output.impression: Structurally normal heart on cardiac MRI. Normal biventricular size and function (LVEF 62%, RVEF 61%). No fibrosis, fatty infiltration, or other abnormality. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Baseline intracardiac recordings: normal sinus node function (SNRT 1050 ms, corrected SNRT 280 ms), normal AH interval (85 ms), normal HV interval (48 ms)
Programmed ventricular stimulation from RV apex: single extrastimulus — no inducible arrhythmia
Double extrastimuli (S1-S2-S3 protocol): non-sustained polymorphic VT of 5 beats at S2=230ms, S3=210ms
Triple extrastimuli: sustained polymorphic VT induced (8 seconds duration) at coupling intervals S2=240ms, S3=220ms, S4=200ms — self-terminating
VT morphology: polymorphic with rapid rate (~280 bpm), consistent with ventricular fibrillation precursor
No monomorphic VT induced
EP study interpreted as POSITIVE — inducible sustained polymorphic VT
output.interpretation: Electrophysiology study reveals inducible sustained polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (8 seconds) with triple extrastimuli programmed stimulation.

Cardiac Channelopathy Gene Panel.value: Pathogenic variant detected: c.4813+1G>A (splice-site mutation, loss of function)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: SCN5A gene Pathogenic variant detected: c.4813+1G>A (splice-site mutation, loss of function) qualitative (H), Overall panel result Positive: pathogenic SCN5A variant qualitative (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Pathogenic SCN5A variant: c.4813+1G>A (splice-site, loss of function) (H)

output.rhythm: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm with fever-correlated ventricular ectopy
findings: Predominant normal sinus rhythm throughout 72-hour recording, average rate 82 bpm (range 58-108 bpm)
Multiple episodes of polymorphic ventricular ectopy correlating with documented fever spikes:
Temperature 38.2°C: isolated PVCs, polymorphic morphology (14 PVCs over 2 hours)
Temperature 38.8°C: ventricular couplets (3 episodes) and one triplet
Temperature 39.1°C: 3-beat run of polymorphic VT at rate 220 bpm, self-terminating
Temperature 39.4°C: ST-segment changes in V1-V2 consistent with Type 1 Brugada pattern appeared on continuous monitoring, concurrent with 5-beat run of polymorphic VT
When afebrile (temperature <38°C): no ventricular ectopy whatsoever, normal sinus rhythm with normal ST segments
No sustained VT or VF during the monitoring period
No AV block, significant pauses, or bradyarrhythmias
Clear dose-response relationship between temperature and arrhythmia burden
output.interpretation: Seventy-two-hour continuous telemetry during hospitalization reveals a striking correlation between fever and ventricular arrhythmia. Polymorphic ventricular ectopy (PVCs, couplets, triplets, and short runs of polymorphic VT up to 5 beats) appear exclusively during febrile periods, with complete resolution of ectopy when the patient is afebrile.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Brugada syndrome with fever-triggered ventricular arrhythmia causing syncope

differential.key_distinguishing: Normal EEG, normal brain MRI, no post-ictal confusion, no tongue biting, no incontinence, all episodes linked exclusively to fever, one episode with documented pulselessness

No typical vasovagal triggers (standing, emotional stress, pain); all episodes during fever; pulseless episode during one event is inconsistent with vasovagal mechanism; family history of sudden death is a red flag
Normal cardiac MRI with no RV dilation, wall motion abnormalities, fatty infiltration, or fibrosis; structurally normal heart excludes ARVC
Normal QTc on multiple ECGs (406-412 ms); fever-triggered pattern is classic Brugada, not LQTS; LQTS syncope is typically triggered by auditory stimuli (LQT2) or exercise/swimming (LQT1), not fever

Adult febrile seizures are exceedingly rare; no seizure activity witnessed; brief twitching is consistent with convulsive syncope; one episode with documented pulselessness confirms cardiac mechanism; normal EEG

optimal_actions.action: Perform ECG during active fever — Brugada pattern may only manifest during febrile states
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initial ECG at 38.6°C shows only subtle non-diagnostic changes; repeat ECG at 39.4°C reveals Type 1 Brugada pattern

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs including electrolytes, cardiac markers, and identify the febrile illness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal electrolytes, normal troponin/BNP, influenza A positive — confirms fever is from a benign infection, not the primary problem

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity given referral pattern of recurrent loss of consciousness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG, no epileptiform discharges — supports cardiac rather than epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural neurological cause
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI — redirects focus to cardiac evaluation

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring and aggressively manage fever with antipyretics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Telemetry reveals polymorphic ventricular ectopy correlating with fever spikes

optimal_actions.action: Perform ajmaline or procainamide provocation test when afebrile to confirm Brugada syndrome diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive ajmaline test unmasking Type 1 Brugada pattern in V1-V3

optimal_actions.action: Order cardiac MRI to exclude structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Structurally normal heart — excludes ARVC, HCM, myocarditis

optimal_actions.action: Refer for ICD implantation (class I indication given syncope with spontaneous Type 1 pattern)
Order genetic testing for SCN5A and cascade screening of first-degree family members (brother and daughter)
Educate patient to aggressively treat fever with antipyretics during any illness and seek immediate medical attention

critical_actions: Perform ECG during active fever (Brugada pattern may only appear with fever)
Aggressive fever management with antipyretics in ALL febrile illnesses
Perform ajmaline/procainamide provocation test when afebrile to confirm
ICD implantation (class I indication given syncope + spontaneous Type 1 pattern)
Genetic testing for SCN5A and cascade screening of family members (brother)
Educate patient to seek immediate care and treat fever aggressively during any illness
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing sodium channel blocking antiarrhythmics (flecainide, propafenone) — these drugs worsen the Brugada phenotype and can trigger VF
Starting antiepileptic drugs for presumed seizures without cardiac workup — convulsive syncope mimics seizures
Dismissing syncope as vasovagal given the red flags (family sudden death, pulseless episode, exclusive fever association)
Discharging without continuous monitoring during febrile episode — patient is at high risk for VF while febrile
key_reasoning_points: Syncope exclusively during febrile episodes is a hallmark presentation of Brugada syndrome — fever unmasks the ECG pattern by further reducing sodium channel function in patients with SCN5A loss-of-function mutations

Fever accelerates voltage-gated sodium channel inactivation kinetics, reducing the already diminished INa current in Brugada patients below the threshold for stable repolarization, creating the substrate for phase-2 reentry and polymorphic VT/VF

Southeast Asian (Filipino) ethnicity has the highest prevalence of Brugada syndrome worldwide — it is a leading cause of sudden unexplained nocturnal death syndrome (SUNDS, known as 'bangungot' in the Philippines)
Family history of sudden unexplained death at age 42 in a Filipino man is highly suspicious for Brugada syndrome — the father likely died from fever-triggered or nocturnal VF

Normal resting ECG does NOT exclude Brugada — up to 60% of Brugada patients have intermittent or concealed ECG patterns that require fever or pharmacologic provocation (ajmaline, procainamide, flecainide) to unmask
Briefly pulseless episode during the COVID infection event strongly suggests polymorphic VT or VF as the mechanism, not simple vasovagal syncope — this is a cardiac arrest survivor
Brother's febrile syncope in Manila suggests familial Brugada syndrome — cascade genetic screening is essential and potentially life-saving

red_herrings.data_point: Normal resting ECG with only subtle V1-V2 changes
red_herrings.intended_effect: May lead clinician to dismiss cardiac etiology entirely based on a 'normal' ECG
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Brugada ECG pattern can be concealed at baseline and only manifest during fever or with pharmacologic provocation; subtle V1-V2 changes should raise suspicion in the right clinical context

red_herrings.data_point: Normal brain MRI and normal EEG
red_herrings.intended_effect: May lead to a 'diagnosis of exclusion' as vasovagal syncope after ruling out neurological causes, providing false reassurance
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Normal neuro workup should redirect diagnostic focus to cardiac etiology, especially in a patient with fever-exclusive syncope, pulseless episodes, and family history of sudden death

red_herrings.intended_effect: May attribute syncope to influenza-related dehydration, hypotension, or febrile seizure rather than recognizing fever as the trigger for a channelopathy
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: The specific illness is irrelevant — the fever itself is the unmasking agent for Brugada syndrome. Any febrile illness would trigger the same arrhythmic events.

clinical_pearls: Brugada syndrome is the leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young adults in Southeast Asia, where it is known as SUNDS (Sudden Unexplained Nocturnal Death Syndrome) or 'bangungot' in the Philippines, 'lai tai' in Thailand, and 'pokkuri' in Japan
Fever is the most common trigger for unmasking concealed Brugada syndrome and can precipitate VF — up to 2% of febrile patients with Brugada syndrome experience arrhythmic events
A negative resting ECG has very poor sensitivity for Brugada syndrome — serial ECGs during fever or pharmacologic provocation testing is required when clinical suspicion is high
Convulsive syncope (brief myoclonic jerks from cerebral hypoperfusion) occurs in up to 12% of syncopal episodes and is commonly misdiagnosed as epileptic seizure
SCN5A loss-of-function mutations are found in 20-25% of genotyped Brugada patients; the mutation has autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance and expressivity
diagnostic_traps: Normal resting ECG creating false reassurance — the initial ECG during mild fever shows only subtle, non-diagnostic changes
Prior syncopal episodes attributed to vasovagal or orthostatic mechanism without recognizing the fever-exclusive pattern
Brief twitching during syncope being misinterpreted as seizure activity rather than convulsive syncope
Influenza infection being accepted as the 'cause' of syncope through dehydration/hypotension rather than recognizing fever as a channelopathy trigger
Lack of awareness of Brugada syndrome's high prevalence in Southeast Asian (especially Filipino) populations
